title: Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Kaitiakitanga in Action / The Mangakōtukutuku Stream: Guardianship in Practice
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the local awa (stream) of Mangakōtukutuku, which flows through our rohe (territory) of Ngāti Kahungunu. Students will explore the pūrākau (traditional story) of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden who is said to watch over the stream, and connect this to modern kaitiakitanga (guardianship) practices. The lesson will incorporate mātauranga Māori specific to our local environment, including traditional names for native fish species found in the awa and the significance of the stream to our local hapū.
learningObjectives: Identify and use at least 10 te reo Māori vocabulary words related to freshwater ecosystems and kaitiakitanga in spoken and written contexts.
Explain the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi and its connection to the Mangakōtukutuku stream using appropriate te reo Māori sentence structures.
Collaborate in small groups to conduct a simple health assessment of a local section of the Mangakōtukutuku stream, recording observations in te reo Māori.
Create a short pepeha (introduction) that includes the Mangakōtukutuku stream as a significant local landmark.

activities.title: He Pepeha mō Te Awa (Introduction to Our Stream)
activities.description: Students will learn and create their own pepeha that includes the Mangakōtukutuku stream. They will practice introducing themselves and their connection to the awa using appropriate tikanga and te reo Māori structures, focusing on correct pronunciation and cultural protocols.

activities.title: Te Pūrākau o Hinepūkohurangi (The Story of the Mist Maiden)
activities.description: Through interactive storytelling with local kaumātua (elders), students will learn the traditional story of Hinepūkohurangi and her connection to our stream. They will then work in pairs to retell key elements of the story using te reo Māori, creating simple storyboards with Māori captions.

activities.title: Te Tirohanga o Te Kaitiaki (The Guardian's Perspective)
activities.description: Students will take a guided field trip to the Mangakōtukutuku stream with local kaitiaki (guardians). They will learn traditional and scientific methods for assessing stream health, recording observations of water clarity, native vegetation, and wildlife sightings using te reo Māori observation sheets.

activities.title: Ngā Ingoa o Ngā Ika (Names of Our Fish)
activities.description: Students will research and learn the Māori names for native fish species found in our stream (such as tuna - eel, inanga - whitebait, and kōura - freshwater crayfish). They will create illustrated flashcards with Māori names, descriptions, and cultural significance, then practice using these terms in sentences about conservation.

activities.title: He Mahi Kaitiakitanga (Guardianship Action Project)
activities.description: Working in small groups, students will design and implement a simple kaitiakitanga project for the stream (such as planting native riparian vegetation, creating educational signs in te reo Māori, or organizing a stream clean-up). They will present their projects to the class using te reo Māori to explain their actions and intended outcomes.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to share pūrākau and traditional knowledge
Te Aka Māori Dictionary online (maoridictionary.co.nz) for vocabulary development
NIWA's Freshwater Fish Identification Guide with Māori names
Mangakōtukutuku Stream Care Group members for guided field visit and practical activities

tasks: Create and present a pepeha that includes the Mangakōtukutuku stream
Complete a te reo Māori observation sheet during the stream visit
Participate in a group presentation about their kaitiakitanga action project using te reo Māori
Create illustrated flashcards with Māori names and descriptions of 5 native stream species

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communicate about relationships between people, places, and things
Te Reo Māori: Use cultural knowledge to communicate understanding
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Science: Develop an understanding of the nature of science and its relationship to te ao Māori
